name.text: Week 16 – Q6: Wireless Power Transfer Efficiency Design

questiontext.text:
Scenario: Your team is developing a wireless charging pad for electric vehicles (EVs). The system uses inductive power transfer between a transmitter coil embedded in the ground and a receiver coil mounted under the vehicle.

The project specification requires at least 95% power transfer efficiency. Your task: evaluate the current design and determine whether the operating frequency meets this target.

System parameters:

  • Transmitter coil (ground): \(L_1 = {@L1_uH@}\;\mu\text{H}\), \(R_1 = {@R1@}\;\Omega\)
  • Receiver coil (vehicle): \(L_2 = {@L2_uH@}\;\mu\text{H}\), \(R_2 = {@R2@}\;\Omega\)
  • Coupling coefficient: \(k = {@k_val@}\)
  • Operating frequency: \(f = {@f_kHz@}\) kHz

Assume resonant matching (reactive parts cancelled by tuning capacitors).

Part A — System Characterization

(a) Calculate the mutual inductance \(M = k\sqrt{L_1 L_2}\).

\(M = \) [[input:ans1]] [[validation:ans1]] H

Enter in henries, e.g., 2.3e-7. Scientific notation is accepted.

(b) Calculate the coupling reactance \(\omega M\) at the operating frequency.

\(\omega M = \) [[input:ans2]] [[validation:ans2]] \(\Omega\)

Enter in ohms, e.g., 42.0.

(c) Calculate the power transfer efficiency at resonance:

\[\eta = \frac{(\omega M)^2}{R_1 R_2 + (\omega M)^2}\]

\(\eta = \) [[input:ans3]] [[validation:ans3]]

Enter as a decimal between 0 and 1, e.g., 0.29.

Part B — Design Optimization

(d) The project requires \(\eta \geq 0.95\). At what minimum frequency \(f_{\min}\) (in kHz) would this efficiency target be met?

Rearrange the efficiency formula to solve for \(\omega\), then convert to \(f\).

\(f_{\min} = \) [[input:ans4]] [[validation:ans4]] kHz

Enter in kHz, e.g., 73.5.

Part C — System Understanding

(e) Which course modules are involved in the design of this WPT system?

[[input:ans5]] [[validation:ans5]]

(f) In practice, the vehicle may not park perfectly centered over the charging pad. If the coupling coefficient \(k\) is reduced (due to misalignment), what happens to the efficiency at the same operating frequency?

[[input:ans6]] [[validation:ans6]]

(g) This system transfers energy wirelessly, yet it does not involve electromagnetic wave propagation (Module M4). Why not?

[[input:ans7]] [[validation:ans7]]

generalfeedback.text:
Worked Solution

Given: \(L_1 = {@L1_uH@}\;\mu\text{H}\), \(L_2 = {@L2_uH@}\;\mu\text{H}\), \(R_1 = {@R1@}\;\Omega\), \(R_2 = {@R2@}\;\Omega\), \(k = {@k_val@}\), \(f = {@f_kHz@}\) kHz.

(a) Mutual inductance:

\[M = k\sqrt{L_1 L_2} = {@k_val@} \times \sqrt{{@L1_uH@} \times {@L2_uH@}} \;\mu\text{H} = {@M_uH_disp@}\;\mu\text{H} = {@tans1@} \text{ H}\]

(b) Coupling reactance:

\[\omega = 2\pi f = 2\pi \times {@f_kHz@} \times 10^3 \text{ rad/s}\]

\[\omega M = {@float(omega_val)@} \times {@tans1@} = {@tans2@}\;\Omega\]

(c) Power transfer efficiency:

\[\eta = \frac{(\omega M)^2}{R_1 R_2 + (\omega M)^2} = \frac{({@tans2@})^2}{{@R1@} \times {@R2@} + ({@tans2@})^2} = {@tans3@}\]

This is {@float(tans3*100)@}% efficiency at resonance with ideal alignment.

(d) Minimum frequency for 95% efficiency:

Set \(\eta \geq 0.95\):

\[\frac{(\omega M)^2}{R_1 R_2 + (\omega M)^2} \geq 0.95\]

\[(\omega M)^2 \geq 0.95\,(R_1 R_2 + (\omega M)^2)\]

\[0.05\,(\omega M)^2 \geq 0.95\,R_1 R_2\]

\[(\omega M)^2 \geq 19\,R_1 R_2\]

\[\omega \geq \frac{\sqrt{19\,R_1 R_2}}{M}\]

\[f_{\min} = \frac{1}{2\pi}\,\frac{\sqrt{19\,R_1 R_2}}{M} = {@tans4@} \text{ kHz}\]

(e) Cross-module connections:

  • M1 (Circuits): Power transfer, equivalent circuit analysis
  • M2 (Laplace): Resonant matching: \(f_0 = 1/(2\pi\sqrt{LC})\)
  • M3 (Magnetics): Mutual inductance \(M = k\sqrt{L_1 L_2}\), Faraday's law for induced EMF

At Qi frequencies (100-200 kHz), \(\lambda \approx 3\) km \(\gg\) coil size, so wave effects (M4) are negligible. WPT uses M1 + M2 + M3.

(f) Reducing \(k\) reduces \(M = k\sqrt{L_1 L_2}\), which reduces \(\omega M\). Since \(\eta = (\omega M)^2/(R_1 R_2 + (\omega M)^2)\), a smaller \(\omega M\) reduces the numerator relative to the denominator — efficiency drops. This is why parking alignment matters for wireless EV charging: even a few centimetres of offset can significantly reduce \(k\) and thus the charging efficiency.

(g) At \(f = {@f_kHz@}\) kHz, the free-space wavelength is \(\lambda = c/f \approx {@float(c0/(f_kHz*10^3)/1000)@}\) km. The coils are only centimetres in size (\(\ell/\lambda \ll 0.01\)). The system operates entirely in the near field: energy is transferred through magnetic coupling (Faraday's law), not through propagating electromagnetic waves. This is the fundamental difference between wireless power transfer and radio communication.

questionnote.text: {@sconcat("L1=", L1_uH, "uH L2=", L2_uH, "uH R1=", R1, " R2=", R2, " k=", k_val, " f=", f_kHz, "kHz eta=", tans3)@}

feedbackvariables.text: err_sum_flag: if abs(M_err_sum) > 0 and abs(ans1 - M_err_sum)/abs(M_err_sum) < 0.06 then 1 else 0;

truefeedback.text: You used \(M = k(L_1 + L_2)\), but the correct formula is \(M = k\sqrt{L_1 L_2}\) (geometric mean, not arithmetic sum).

falsefeedback.text: Incorrect. \(M = k\sqrt{L_1 L_2}\). Make sure to convert \(\mu\text{H}\) to H: multiply by \(10^{-6}\).

feedbackvariables.text: err_noomega_flag: if abs(wM_err_noomega) > 0 and abs(ans2 - wM_err_noomega)/abs(wM_err_noomega) < 0.06 then 1 else 0;

truefeedback.text: You used \(f \times M\) instead of \(\omega \times M\). Remember: \(\omega = 2\pi f\), not just \(f\).

falsefeedback.text: Incorrect. \(\omega M = 2\pi f \times M\). Convert kHz to Hz: {@f_kHz@} kHz = {@f_Hz@} Hz.

feedbackvariables.text: /* Check if efficiency > 1 (impossible) */ err_over1_flag: if is(ans3 > 1) then 1 else 0;

truefeedback.text: Your efficiency is greater than 1 (100%), which is physically impossible. Check the formula: \(\eta = (\omega M)^2 / (R_1 R_2 + (\omega M)^2)\). This fraction is always less than 1.

falsefeedback.text: Incorrect. \(\eta = (\omega M)^2 / (R_1 R_2 + (\omega M)^2)\). Use your \(\omega M\) from part (b).

truefeedback.text: You solved for \(\omega_{\min}\) correctly but forgot to convert to \(f\): \(f = \omega/(2\pi)\), not \(f = \omega\).

falsefeedback.text: Incorrect. Rearrange \(\eta \geq 0.95\): \((\omega M)^2 \geq 19\,R_1 R_2\), so \(\omega \geq \sqrt{19\,R_1 R_2}/M\), then \(f = \omega/(2\pi)\). Convert to kHz.

truefeedback.text: Correct. At ~100 kHz, wavelength is ~3 km — far larger than the coil. No wave/radiation effects (M4). WPT uses M1 (circuits), M2 (resonance), and M3 (mutual inductance).

falsefeedback.text: Incorrect. The key question is: does this system need wave analysis (M4)? At {@f_kHz@} kHz, \(\lambda = c/f \approx 3\) km. Since coils are ~cm sized (\(\ll \lambda\)), wave effects are negligible. WPT uses M1 + M2 + M3, not M4.

truefeedback.text: Correct. Lower \(k\) reduces \(M\) and thus \(\omega M\), which directly reduces \(\eta\). This is why parking alignment is important for wireless EV charging.

falsefeedback.text: Incorrect. Look at the efficiency formula: \(\eta = (\omega M)^2/(R_1 R_2 + (\omega M)^2)\). Since \(M = k\sqrt{L_1 L_2}\), reducing \(k\) reduces \(M\), which reduces \(\omega M\), which reduces the numerator — so efficiency drops.

truefeedback.text: Correct. At {@f_kHz@} kHz, \(\lambda \approx\) 3 km — far larger than the coils. The system operates in the near field through magnetic coupling (Faraday's law), not wave propagation.

falsefeedback.text: Incorrect. The key is the wavelength: at {@f_kHz@} kHz, \(\lambda = c/f \approx 3\) km. The coils are centimetres in size — thousands of times smaller than \(\lambda\). At this scale, there are no propagating waves. Energy transfers through near-field magnetic coupling (mutual inductance), not radiation.

hint.text: Mutual inductance: \(M = k\sqrt{L_1 L_2}\) — note it is the geometric mean (square root of the product), not the arithmetic sum. Convert \(\mu\text{H}\) to H by multiplying by \(10^{-6}\). Then the coupling reactance \(\omega M = 2\pi f \times M\) tells you how strongly the coils interact — larger \(\omega M\) means more efficient power transfer.

Design optimization: Set \(\eta = 0.95\) and rearrange: \((\omega M)^2 \geq 19\,R_1 R_2\). Solving for \(\omega\) gives \(\omega \geq \sqrt{19\,R_1 R_2}/M\). Then \(f = \omega/(2\pi)\) — do not forget the \(2\pi\). Convert to kHz by dividing by 1000.

System understanding: Efficiency depends on \((\omega M)^2\) in the numerator. Anything that reduces \(\omega M\) — lower \(k\) from misalignment, or lower frequency — reduces efficiency. For the wave question: compute \(\lambda = c/f\) and compare to the coil size. If \(\lambda\) is thousands of times larger, there are no wave effects — the system operates through near-field magnetic coupling.
